title: Chapter 24: The Meal
content: Sarah hit 50% on Day Twenty-One.
I know because the system practically threw a parade—a fireworks display of golden light on the watch face that was visible from across the room and made everyone look up from what they were doing.

**ROMANCE MILESTONE: SARAH MITCHELL — 50%**
**STATUS: Deep Emotional Connection Established**
**REWARD: Medical Supply Kit (Rare) — Unlocked**
**NEXT MILESTONE: 75% — Physical Intimacy Threshold**
**NOTE: Approach organically. Forced progression will trigger Hollow Heart.**
"Your watch is doing something," Sarah said from the kitchen, where she'd been helping Rose inventory the medical bay.
"It does that. Don't worry about it."

She gave me a look—*we're going to talk about this later*—and returned to the inventory. I made a mental note to explain the system's milestones before she found out some other way. Honesty was the foundation of whatever we were building, and I'd already leaned too hard on the *don't worry about it* deflection.
But first: dinner.

It had become our ritual. Every night, the whole group gathered at the titanium table for a shared meal—whatever the system's quests had produced that day, supplemented by the hydroponic garden's growing harvest. It was the one moment when we weren't survivors, when we were just people eating food together, and the simplicity of it had become the anchor that held us together in a world that wanted to tear us apart.
Tonight, though, I wanted something more.

"Sarah," I said after the dishes were cleared and the others had drifted to their evening routines—Rose to her lab, Mia to the roof, Rick and Maria to their quiet corner, Jessica to the entertainment system with Boss in her lap. "Can I make you dinner? Just the two of us."
She paused in the act of stacking bowls. "Like a date?"
"Like a meal. Between two people who've been through hell and haven't had a chance to breathe."

She looked at me—searching my face for the game, the angle, the system's invisible hand steering her toward a quest reward. She was still looking, even now, even after everything. Derek had taught her that kindness always came with conditions, and unlearning that lesson was going to take more than good intentions.
"No system stuff," I said, reading her expression. "No quests, no rewards, no affection tracking. Just food and conversation. I'll even take the watch off."
"You can't take it off. It's fused to your skin."
"Fine. I'll cover it with a towel."
That almost-smile again. The real one, not the ghost—the one that started in her eyes and worked its way down.
"Okay," she said. "But I'm choosing the movie afterward."
"Deal."
I made steak.

Not system steak—I'd been saving this, a premium cut from the daily quest rewards that I'd stashed in the back of the refrigeration unit, wrapped in a cloth like a treasure. I'd never cooked steak before; my culinary repertoire topped out at microwave burritos and instant ramen. But the safe house had a real stove and a cast-iron skillet, and I'd watched enough YouTube cooking videos in my pre-apocalypse life to have a theoretical understanding of searing.

The results were... edible. More than edible—actually pretty good, with a seared crust and a pink center and the kind of buttery richness that made every bite feel like a victory against the void. I served it with roasted potatoes from the hydroponic garden and a glass of wine that the system had produced as a "relationship milestone bonus."
Sarah ate slowly, savoring every bite, her eyes closing at the first taste of meat that wasn't canned or scavenged.
"This is really good," she said, and the surprise in her voice told me more about her recent past than any conversation could.
"I had a good teacher. The internet, I mean. Before it died."
"Thank you," she said, and she wasn't talking about the food.
"For what?"

"For seeing me. For not treating me like a quest." She set down her fork. "I know the system tracks my affection. I know you're getting a reward for this. But I also know that you saved me before you knew about the titanium safe house. You came for me because you heard a woman in trouble, and that matters more than any number on a watch."
My throat tightened. "It's not a number, Sarah. Not to me."

"I know. That's what makes it complicated." She took a breath. "I spent a week with a man who saw me as property. Before that, I spent years in a world that saw me as a future doctor—a title, not a person. You're the first person who's seen me as both. A person *and* a professional. Someone who can contribute and someone who needs help."
"You don't need to earn your place here," I said. "You already have one."

"That's what I keep forgetting." She looked at me, and her eyes were wet—not crying, not yet, but close. "In Derek's world, everything had a price. Safety cost obedience. Food cost submission. Nothing was free. And here you are, giving me steak and wine and a bedroom with a real mattress, and asking for nothing in return."

"I'm not asking for nothing," I admitted. "I'm hoping you'll stay. I'm hoping you'll keep cooking with me and arguing with Mia and teaching Jessica how to suture. I'm hoping you'll be part of this—this ridiculous, impossible, end-of-the-world family we're building."
"Family," she repeated.
"That's what it feels like. Doesn't it?"

She looked at the closed door of the medical bay, where Rose was analyzing Luna's blood. At the entertainment corner, where Jessica and Boss were watching something animated. At the window, where Mia was silhouetted against the night sky, keeping watch. At the quiet corner where Rick and Maria were talking in low voices, two survivors finding their footing.
"Family," she said again, and this time it sounded less like a question and more like an acceptance.
"Sarah—"

"Don't." She held up a hand. "I know what you're going to say, and I'm not ready. Not yet. I need time. I need to feel like myself again before I can be someone's... whatever. But I want you to know that I'm not rejecting you. I'm protecting myself. And I hope you can understand the difference."
I could. I did.
"Take all the time you need," I said. "I'll be here."
"Promise?"
"Promise."
She reached across the table and took my hand—the same point of contact we'd shared during Die Hard, but charged with a different energy now. Softer. Deliberate.
"Thank you for dinner," she said.
"Thank you for staying."
**AFFECTION UPDATE: SARAH MITCHELL — 56%**
The number climbed in silence, and neither of us mentioned it.
We didn't need to.
The connection was real. The system was just counting what we already knew.

Later that night, after Sarah had gone to bed and I was lying awake in the darkness, Luna appeared beside me. She moved like always—silent, fluid, appearing from nowhere to occupy the space she'd claimed as hers.
"Good dinner," she whispered.
"You were eavesdropping."
"Listening. Different." She lay down beside me, not touching but close enough to feel her warmth. "You love her."
It wasn't a question.
"I care about her," I said carefully.
"You love her," Luna repeated. "And you love me. And that is..." She searched for the word. "Complicated."
"Yeah. It is."
"In the old world, people loved one person. One at a time. That was the rule." She paused. "The old world is dead. Maybe the old rules are dead too."
I turned to look at her. In the dim light, her eyes caught the glow of the watch—those dark brown irises that had gone from dead-black to something approaching life.
"Are you okay with this?" I asked.

"I don't know," she said honestly. "The zombie in me wants to bite anything that threatens my bond. The person in me knows that biting is wrong." A tiny smile. "I am learning. So are you. We learn together."
"Together," I said.
"Always," she agreed, and her hand found mine in the dark, and this time there was nothing jealous or territorial about it—just the simple, profound certainty of someone who'd chosen to stay.
I fell asleep holding her hand, thinking about love and rules and the freedom of a world where the old certainties had burned and new ones were still taking shape.
Tomorrow, we'd keep building.
Tonight, we'd rest.
